words: SAM SCHRAGER: This interview with Glen Gilder was recorded on June 17, 1975
GLEN GILDER: I remember is that we hauled our wheat in there, dumped and
took our empty sacks home and refilled 'em. While as most when you
hauled it in they kept the sacks. Because they had to- it was before
the days of elevators, and long before, but there they did have bins and
they kept 'em. Now, Idon't know whether anybody ever run that before
mill. Art's dad did or not. That's before my time but his dad run thatA
there for years.

speaker: SS
words: Did it run on water power?

speaker: GG
words: Altogether, they didn't have anything else. 1 think in the later years,

speaker: GG
words: that they did convert it to electricity, but Inever seen that. I had gone

speaker: GG
words: from Palouse before they did it.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have all the power they needed to do all the grinding they wanted?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah they seemed to, they seemed to. They had abig dam there fourteen feet

speaker: GG
words: high, Iguess, maybe higher. That pond used to go back up the river there

speaker: GG
words: for a mile or more.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have big grinding stones?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah, it was a stone mill, although Inever any more than just one time

speaker: GG
words: walked up and looked around, but then I didn't understand anything about

speaker: GG
words: it. It was all Greek to me. But he made good flour. We'd haul our own

speaker: GG
words: wheat in there and trade with him. Take the flour home- a whole year's

speaker: GG
words: supply at one shot.

speaker: SS
words: How much would that be?

speaker: GG
words: Twenty sacks or better. Fifty pound sacks. Twenty, twenty-five. Idon't

speaker: GG
words: know. When you take your wheat in there you trade 'em a sack of wheat, I

speaker: GG
words: believe for a sack of flour. And then you got with that all the bran

speaker: GG
words: that came off it and farina, all the by-products. You got that back, they

speaker: GG
words: didn't want it. Feed part of it to the hogs and part of it to the kids

speaker: GG
words: and make quite a lot of use out of it.

speaker: SS
words: What was the charge?

speaker: GG
words: There was no charge. A straight across trade: one sack of wheat, one

speaker: GG
words: sack of flour. But then, a sack of wheat would make more than a sack of

speaker: GG
words: flour, that was all there was to that. He probably more than likely gave

speaker: GG
words: us half. The flour that come out of it weighed a hundred— no fifty pounds

speaker: GG
words: and the sack of wheat that went in there weighed about a hundred and ten,

speaker: GG
words: a good sack a hundred and twenty. But we were darn happy to do it, be

speaker: GG
words: cause wheat wasn't very high and neither was flour, as far as that goes,

speaker: GG
words: probably a dollar and a quarter to a dollar and a half. Idon't remember

speaker: GG
words: but it was in that neighborhood.

speaker: SS
words: Did that mill buy a lot of wheat? Or did you just trade 'em?

speaker: GG
words: No, no, no. We drove in there from north of Tord with— we gener

speaker: GG
words: ally hauled with two teams. I was just a little punk of a kid, but I

speaker: GG
words: could drive horses. uncle would drive the team ahead of me and

speaker: GG
words: then I'd follow him with mine. And we had on about forty-five sacks to

speaker: GG
words: the team, but when we'd get down there, there might be a line of teams

speaker: GG
words: lined up there for, oh, clear out across the main road to Potlatch— there

speaker: GG
words: might be ten or fifteen or twenty four-horse teams in there. No they

speaker: GG
words: handled a good volume of business. They bought or traded or any way you

speaker: GG
words: wanted to deal, they didn't care. It made a pretty good deal for the

speaker: GG
words: farmers, I guess, because they got the sack back and that was quite an ex

speaker: GG
words: pense. And we got market price for the wheat just the same as if they'd

speaker: GG
words: kept the sacks. The old man was a good business man and he was a good

speaker: GG
words: miller. Knew his stuff. It was good flour.

speaker: SS
words: Where do you think the mill's market would have been for their grain?

speaker: GG
words: Well, There was a ready market. They would buy it on the open mar

speaker: GG
words: ket, Spokane or anyplace else, but I'll just bet you that they pretty near

speaker: GG
words: ate that up right around Palouse,I'll just bet you they did. 'Cause

speaker: GG
words: you bring in flour from other areas, it more than likely cost more, it

speaker: GG
words: certainly would cost more to produce it and ship it because of the added

speaker: GG
words: things that entered in there, but I'll bet you that it was almost entirely

speaker: GG
words: eaten around Palouse, but I don't know that.

speaker: SS
words: Palouse was really a pretty big town to the - important town to the Pal

speaker: SS
words: ouse River area?

speaker: GG
words: Well, until they put the mill in there, there wasn't anything from Palouse

speaker: GG
words: to the head of the river. A stage stop and post office, and oh, a few

speaker: GG
words: but very few. Small little stores that weren't not near as big

speaker: GG
words: as this living room, but they'd post office and store, and what-haveyou

speaker: GG
words: in there. There was astage stop at Ky Ford, there was astage

speaker: GG
words: stop next to Princeton, the old town, Hanson, and then Idon't think there

speaker: GG
words: was another one until you got to Wood. And then you had to go

speaker: GG
words: over the mountain to Emida, and they were all served by the same stage line,

speaker: GG
words: And I think they turned around at Emida and came back, I'm not positive

speaker: GG
words: of that, but I think he did. And another met him there and went on to

speaker: GG
words: St. Maries and the St. Joe River.

speaker: SS
words: Would this be a daily run from Palouse?

speaker: GG
words: I don't believe they made a daily run, I think about twice a week. When

speaker: GG
words: the sawmill was being built and come in there, Art Craig was running it,

speaker: GG
words: the stage, and there'd be a bunch of men come in to Palouse, you know,

speaker: GG
words: and wanted to go up the river for the woods work or railroad or whatever.

speaker: GG
words: So Art couldn't take 'em all, but he'd charge 'em just as much to take

speaker: GG
words: the packsack and bed,and they were happy to get it done, and then they

speaker: GG
words: walked and the wagon hauled their stuff, plus the groceries and mail. Fe

speaker: GG
words: male passengers, they couldn't walk, kids (Chuckles).

speaker: SS
words: He must have had a pretty good business.

speaker: GG
words: He did. He did. And then after this excitement was all over and the mill

speaker: GG
words: was in and the railroad was built, why that ruined it. So he put up a

speaker: GG
words: livery barn there in Princeton, that's in the new town, and spent the

speaker: GG
words: rest of his life there running that livery stable. Horses, saddle horses,

speaker: GG
words: driving horses, freight horses, anything you wanted, they had it.

speaker: SS
words: Did he start in Princeton, or did he start at Potlatch?

speaker: GG
words: No, he started at Palouse for the first of it. Whether there was anybody

speaker: GG
words: else run a stageline up there, I don't really know, Edna could tell you.

speaker: GG
words: She knew it farther back than I did and she knew it much better, 'cause

speaker: GG
words: her dad ran the post office there at Woodfell

speaker: SS
words: Iwonder about how long it would take him to get to Woodfell ? That

speaker: SS
words: was an overnight stop for travelers but probably not for the—

speaker: GG
words: I think probably. That'd be about twenty-eight miles, and I think they

speaker: GG
words: just probably figured on doing that. A good team'd do it easily, but if

speaker: GG
words: they were loaded pretty heavy, why it might be quite a task. I have just

speaker: GG
words: an idea that that was an overnight stop right there. And the next day they

speaker: GG
words: went on to Emida, stayed overnight and came back. It'd take four days,—

speaker: GG
words: three or four days to do that, near as I can figure, I certainly don't rem

speaker: GG
words: ember.

speaker: SS
words: For a while I've heard there was a stage that went up to— at least as far

speaker: SS
words: as I thought further up the Palouse River.

speaker: GG
words: They did, that's right, it went from Harvard up the Palouse River to those

speaker: GG
words: placer mines up there, but I don't know who it is. I don't know anything

speaker: GG
words: about it, don't remember a thing about the)fschedule. But then there wasn't

speaker: GG
words: any other transportation, it was just that horse. And there was lots and

speaker: GG
words: lots of littlesftagelines all over the country, the same as there are highft

speaker: GG
words: ways and railroads now. And then the railroads come in and that cut them

speaker: GG
words: out of their main line. Have you ever read The Shadowy St. Joe?

speaker: SS
words: I've seen it though.

speaker: GG
words: You could get a lot of help out of that book. The first half of it at any

speaker: GG
words: rate.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever hear of the old Hoodoo Trail?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah. Went from Johnson's over the hump to the head of the North Fork of

speaker: GG
words: the Palouse. Pack trail. And, by the way, Jake Johnson used to run that

speaker: GG
words: packstring in there, over that trail.

speaker: SS
words: Where was his place?

speaker: GG
words: Woodfell.

speaker: SS
words: His place was at Woodfell?

speaker: GG
words: That was Woodfell. He ran the post office and stage stop there. Well, he

speaker: GG
words: was enterprising, he was aworker, and so was the rest of his family. Fact

speaker: GG
words: of the matter is, all families were at that time. They had to be.

speaker: SS
words: This trail— old Hoodoo Trail, it went up and managed to pass by or close

speaker: SS
words: to a lot of the claims.

speaker: GG
words: Well, the claims and the Hoodoos were pretty well bunched on the North Fork,

speaker: GG
words: and whether they had the distributing point or not, Idon't know. But

speaker: GG
words: he probably went as far as he had mail or groceries, or whatever to go up

speaker: GG
words: there and then turned around and come back. He'd make that trip up and

speaker: GG
words: back in a day. You don't know exactly where Woodfell is, do you?

speaker: SS
words: I do, yeah, it's by Harvard there.

speaker: GG
words: It went right straight east, right over the hills.

speaker: SS
words: Up near Bald Mountain.

speaker: GG
words: Yes, I think it probably got that high, but maybe not. I've been over it-

speaker: GG
words: No I don't think so, although there was a trail over Bald Mountain. I

speaker: GG
words: think that his pack trails to the Palouse was down lower.

speaker: SS
words: I've been reading a little bit about Henry Plummer lately. He really does

speaker: SS
words: sound like a big thief. Real desperado.

speaker: GG
words: He was. No doubt about it. And when I was a kid they claimed that those

speaker: GG
words: cabins at ark were used by him at different times in his horse business.

speaker: GG
words: He had a pretty good horse business. He drove 'em both ways from Alder

speaker: GG
words: Gulch and that area in there to Tekoajin the Spokane area, and then he'd

speaker: GG
words: load up another bunch from down here; he'd sell the ones he got in the East

speaker: GG
words: and he'd sell 'em down here ande'd take another bunch back there and sell

speaker: GG
words: them. They used to claim that that cabin was used by him at different times

speaker: GG
words: and we know that he was in and out of Lewiston real frequently, so there's

speaker: GG
words: little doubt it.

speaker: SS
words: Was it just a straight horse thief operation?

speaker: GG
words: No, he wasn't particular, he'd steal anything. (Chuckles) That's what they

speaker: GG
words: hung him for, was stealing gold! No, he wasn't a specialist, he had a

speaker: GG
words: varied business. He wasn't particular,he'd kill a Chinaman just as quick

speaker: GG
words: as he would a white man. Thought he had a couple of dollars or a gold watch

speaker: GG
words: on him he was doomed. Been a lot written on him, lot of it good. Vardis

speaker: GG
words: Fisher, I think had a pretty good book on him. I thought it was good, any

speaker: GG
words: way. I don't know what the name of it was, don't even remember very much

speaker: GG
words: about it, 'cause it probably could be fifty years since Iread it. Idon't

speaker: GG
words: know.

speaker: SS
words: Were there any other guys in the country who were noted for—?

speaker: GG
words: Wellll, yes, but I don't as I could quote you any names on em. I

speaker: GG
words: never knew any of 'em, as far as I know, I didn't know any of 'em. But it

speaker: GG
words: was quite acommon thing.You see, there was Indians around here, Old Mo

speaker: GG
words: and Spokane Garry and all those fellows. They were millionaires in horses.

speaker: GG
words: Colonel George Wright,killed out there this side of the river from Harrison,

speaker: GG
words: they killed eight hundred head up there, but that didn't hurt 'em. They

speaker: GG
words: thought they was just putting them out of business: well, they did put 'em

speaker: GG
words: out of business, alright. They killed eight hundred head, and as far as

speaker: GG
words: the horses are concerned, that didn't hurt 'em. Old Mo had lots of hor

speaker: GG
words: ses, and he pastured 'em down here at Kendrick, Ritzville- he had abig

speaker: GG
words: outfit there at Spokane Falls— not Spokane Falls, Palouse Falls.

speaker: SS
words: Is MoX Mo the same Indian who used to be up around B°vill?

speaker: GG
words: Yes. But that was only just for berries or hunting. He kind of stayed a

speaker: GG
words: little farther West because horses was his business.

speaker: SS
words: How did he happen to be so well known?

speaker: GG
words: He was so big in his business. He was honest, there was no horse stealing

speaker: GG
words: with him. And if a man needed a horse, and he knew it and he had any res

speaker: GG
words: pect for the man at all, he'd give him ahorse, he didn't think anything

speaker: GG
words: of it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he took his herds up here in the summertime?

speaker: GG
words: He didn't move them a great deal, he just let 'em move themselves. He didn't

speaker: GG
words: have to, there was always feed for them. I've heard and read estimates

speaker: GG
words: about what he had, but- lot of horses. On my dad's homestead down there,

speaker: GG
words: at La Crosse, it was nothing to daily see horses that were wild. There

speaker: GG
words: wasn't anybody owned 'em. My dad caught oh, possibly ten head of them dur

speaker: GG
words: ing the time he was there. He caught one little mare, I don't think she

speaker: GG
words: ever did weigh over, oh, possibly eight hundred pounds, and bred her to a

speaker: GG
words: pretty fair horse, got a thousand, eleven hundred pound colt out of her,

speaker: GG
words: and kept right on with that same blood there for a good many years, I don't

speaker: GG
words: know how many. I don't know how many colts they had with that blood in 'em.

speaker: GG
words: They had others, too, but when he left that homestead down there and come

speaker: GG
words: up to Palouse he sold her and I had a good bawl over it. I'd never ridden

speaker: GG
words: her, or don't think anybody else ever did; tried it, Kdidn't work. But I

speaker: GG
words: bawled 'cause she was part of the family. She'd been around there five

speaker: GG
words: or six years, you know.

speaker: SS
words: She'd just been one of the Indian horses?

speaker: GG
words: Uh-huh. She was Well, after the Indians were corralled and put on these reservations

speaker: GG
words: and so badly whipped, they just left the horses— they just reverted, they

speaker: GG
words: weren't anybody's after that, because the Indian couldn't claim 'em, he

speaker: GG
words: couldn't take care of 'em, so they were just wild horses. And that's where

speaker: GG
words: all the wild horses came from, the Indians had the first title to 'em.

speaker: GG
words: There's one of these Nez Perce Indians, according to the history went clear

speaker: GG
words: down into Texas and brought two old— they got old anyway, I don't know how

speaker: GG
words: old they were, but they brought back two or three horses. That's where the

speaker: GG
words: flez Perces first got their horses. Well, I think that Hatley got it from

speaker: GG
words: Frank, but that's where I read it. The best part was from book. I was wondering what

speaker: GG
words: he called it, Cayuse, or what?

speaker: SS
words: Do you think we just took the Indians' land away from 'em? Did we do them

speaker: SS
words: a real injustice or not?

speaker: GG
words: Yes, we certainly did do them an injustice and we still are, definitely.

speaker: GG
words: But, on the other hand, at the time that all this battling was going on

speaker: GG
words: it was needless- the Indian wouldn't have hurt anybody or anything if the

speaker: GG
words: White man hadn't showed him how. White men, always, most every In

speaker: GG
words: dian uprising, the White men fired the first shots. That was in Nez Perce

speaker: GG
words: and just about everyplace else. In that massacre there at

speaker: SS
words: Whitman.

speaker: GG
words: Whitman, it was the same thing, and it's always been the same thing. The

speaker: GG
words: White men was first. And I don't see why anybody could blame an Indian for

speaker: GG
words: fighting back. What would happen if the Chinamen come in here today and

speaker: GG
words: tried to take this country by the same method: would we just grin and bear

speaker: GG
words: it, or would we scrap? And it's the same thing to me. I wonder, I guess

speaker: GG
words: I always will wonder, 'cause I'm too damn stubborn, nobody could prove

speaker: GG
words: it to me.

speaker: GG
words: But these missionaries come in here to convert the Indians and educate 'em.

speaker: GG
words: They just worshipped the same God, they just called him by a different name,

speaker: GG
words: well, they called everything else by a different name, that was perfectly

speaker: GG
words: natural. That's the way I feel about it.

speaker: SS
words: Seems they divided the Nez Perce so badly to have some of them be the hea

speaker: SS
words: then and some of them be the Christian Nez Perce, seems like it split the

speaker: SS
words: tribe right down the middle.

speaker: GG
words: Well, it did. Whitman and Spalding came and then a year or two later then

speaker: GG
words: here come De Smet and Stevens and they all had a different religion, and

speaker: GG
words: all told the Indians a different story. Well, gee, who's he gonna believe?

speaker: GG
words: I think they done more harm than good.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have much of a reputation back in the early days?

speaker: GG
words: Well, there was for a while there they did, because they had a problem—

speaker: GG
words: they had no government, so they organized their own. And they got this

speaker: GG
words: Hill and a bunch of those fellows in one bunch and then a little later on

speaker: GG
words: there was another they called Dakota Slim and Blackie, I believe, right

speaker: GG
words: after the first one and they threw out of a upper story window of

speaker: GG
words: a hotel, there in Colfax, and I think that made about a total of about

speaker: GG
words: five and that cleaned the thing up, they didn't need them any more. They

speaker: GG
words: just dissolved. Nobody was ever supposed to know who they were, or any

speaker: GG
words: thing about it, but then, they did. No question about it. I think

speaker: GG
words: served the purpose there, same ashedid in Alder Gulch. THey cleaned the

speaker: GG
words: place up, and it certainly had to be cleaned up.

speaker: SS
words: Alder Gulch?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah. That's where they hung Plummer.

speaker: SS
words: It really was lawless from what people would say when you were a kid, I

speaker: SS
words: mean—

speaker: GG
words: No, when I was a kid, no. They'd gone past that.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when they talked about it.

speaker: GG
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What it had been.

speaker: GG
words: But, on the other hand, no, they weren't, there were just these few that

speaker: GG
words: thought that they were just good to work, so they devised

speaker: GG
words: some other means of making aliving. But most of them: no. There was fewer

speaker: GG
words: outlaws and fewer crooks in those days, as compared to population as

speaker: GG
words: it is in reverse today, there's more crooks and outlaws and thieves in op

speaker: GG
words: eration today, and fewer honest people in the high positions— it's just

speaker: GG
words: the reverse of what it was.

speaker: SS
words: Things have been getting worse and not better.

speaker: GG
words: That's right. But it's due to the fact that there's more population, there's

speaker: GG
words: more money, there's more correspondence, there's more opportunity for them.

speaker: GG
words: I've read, I think it was Dob said, that in the early days aman could

speaker: GG
words: walk out of his cabin, hand his watch on the doorknoband be gone a year,

speaker: GG
words: and come back and it wouldn't have lost a minute, ' it would still be

speaker: GG
words: there. And now-a-days if you done that, when you came back the door wouldn't

speaker: GG
words: be there! And likely not the cabin.

speaker: SS
words: You mean that was true when you grew up? Could one of the neighbors leave

speaker: SS
words: a watch out there?

speaker: GG
words: Inever suppose that happened but Dobj says as an example. There was more

speaker: GG
words: honestyomparably than there is now. There were fewer people. Any

speaker: GG
words: body that was crooked or conniving, he wasn't very popular, (lots of inter

speaker: GG
words: ference).

speaker: GG
words: I've known of two, both of 'em women, that were just bored to deathout

speaker: GG
words: here.

speaker: SS
words: Who were from the East?

speaker: GG
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Would you say that most Easterners fall in love with this country?

speaker: GG
words: They mostly do. Mostly. And California, and south, Nevada and Utah and

speaker: GG
words: down in there, if they ever get a chance they'll come up here to live and

speaker: GG
words: settle, if they could make it so that they could live. Most of 'em only

speaker: GG
words: know one way to live, and so they gotta stay there until they reach re

speaker: GG
words: tirement, and then they're too old, but, if they could, most of 'em would

speaker: GG
words: prefer this Northwest. It must be pretty well ideal. I love the desert

speaker: GG
words: and the mountains, equally. This prairie, Palouse, every darned inch of

speaker: GG
words: it is beautiful. So I don't know. If I had of been on those covered wagon

speaker: GG
words: trains that were goingthrough so much to get to Oregon, now I drive through

speaker: GG
words: Laramie and all that area and into Fort Hallown the Boise Valley, and up

speaker: GG
words: to Vale and up through Baker. Iwouldn't have gone through that, I'd have

speaker: GG
words: 'broke my wagon down and stayed there. There's many places that they went

speaker: GG
words: through that they couldn't have improved it, I don't care where they went.

speaker: GG
words: Climate wise, the Boise Valley, there's production. Chinaman could see it

speaker: GG
words: when he came, that he could irrigate from the abundant water there is there,

speaker: GG
words: why they went through it, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: How do you think this country stacks up with places like that?

speaker: GG
words: Well, it's amystery to me, I don't know. Salmon, Idaho, that's where they

speaker: GG
words: hit the Snake River. There were a few dropped off. And if they went into

speaker: GG
words: cattle or sheep or horses, made tremend ous fortunes. If they tried to

speaker: GG
words: grow grain, it took one generation to clear it up and get it into prod

speaker: GG
words: uction and then it took the next generation to lose it through a bankrupt

speaker: GG
words: sale. And that was the case in the Yakima and Wenatchee Valleys. One

speaker: GG
words: generation to homestead it, and the next generation put it in orchard and

speaker: GG
words: the next generation lost it through foreclosed mortgages.(Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: That didn't happen too bad around here though, did it?

speaker: GG
words: It didn't around right here, but that's just built right into the history

speaker: GG
words: of all that orchard country.

speaker: SS
words: What happened, do you know?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, cost of developing was so big, and the slowness of bringing it into

speaker: GG
words: actual production, one working against the other was just too much to drag

speaker: GG
words: down through the years. Was a lot of 'em made it. They had to know that

speaker: GG
words: they had to get right in and dig, you know, instead of waiting for the orchard

speaker: GG
words: to produce, they had to have something producing between- garden

speaker: GG
words: stuff or alfalfa hay or eggs, or something. They made it, and they made

speaker: GG
words: it good. There was another class that didn't. Idon't know what governed

speaker: GG
words: it. I have no idea.

speaker: SS
words: It seems like around here, too, there were a lot of people that left, didn't

speaker: SS
words: stay, didn't wind up staying. Idon't know why some did and some didn't

speaker: SS
words: here, either. They didn't have to leave here, they could have managed to

speaker: SS
words: make a living, couldn't they?

speaker: GG
words: Most of 'em would have been better off if they'd have the stick-to-itive

speaker: GG
words: enough, all right, lots of perseverance to hang onto these places a little

speaker: GG
words: bit longer, conditions would have got better and they'd made it. Now

speaker: GG
